coredata.dc:title: Alginate based films integrated with nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots and layered clay for active food packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Long
Zuo, Juan
Liu, Yujie
Zheng, Bowen
Dai, Xianglong
Bai, Zeqing
Liu, Yulin
Yao, Jin
coredata.dc:description: The applications of alginate derived from seaweed polysaccharide in food packaging are restricted due to their inherent deficient antibacterial, antioxidant and UV barrier properties. In this study, nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots (NCDs) with active functions (0.5–3 %) and layered clay (1 %) with barrier property were introduced to construct alginate based active films via solution casting method. The results showed that the synthesized spherical NCDs had a particle size of 2–3 nm, and the internal structure of NCDs was similar to graphene, with a large number of active groups (-NH2, -OH, etc.) on the surface. Infrared analysis revealed that NCDs could form strong hydrogen bonds with alginate matrix, which slowed down the deterioration of mechanical properties and reduced the surface wettability. With the addition of NCDs, active functions and surface hydrophobicity of the active films were enhanced significantly (P < 0.05). When the amount of NCDs reached 3 %, UV barrier, antioxidant and antibacterial properties of the active films were increased by 50.0 %, 61.1 % and 70.1 %, respectively. The addition of NCDs could enhance the anti-browning ability of alginate based coatings and extend the shelf life of banana significantly. Therefore, a suitable amount of NCDs (1–2 %) and layered clay (1 %) can synergistically improve comprehensive performance of alginate based films and promote their food packaging application used as active films/inner coatings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alginate
Carbon dots
Layered clay
Active packaging
Antibacterial activity
Antioxidant activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of alginate based active films
Micromorphology and chemical structure of NCDs
Chemical structure of alginate based active films
Surface morphology and hydrophilicity of alginate based active films
Mechanical properties of alginate based active films
Optical properties of alginate based active films
Antioxidant activity of alginate based active films
Antibacterial activity of alginate based active films
Gas barrier properties of alginate based active films
Fresh-keeping property of alginate based active films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52103098 ), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province, China (No. 2021J05265 ), Open fund of Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications ( Xiamen University of Technology ) (No. fma2023006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image), (b) the corresponding particle size distribution histogram and (c) FT-IR spectra of NCDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C 1S spectra, (c) O 1S spectra and (d) N 1S spectra of NCDs.
(a) PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths, (b) UV–Vis absorption, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of NCDs.
FT-IR spectra of alginate based active films.

SEM images of surface for (a) Alg, (b) LA and (c) LA-N1. (d) EDS element distribution of LA-N1. (e) Water contact angle and (f) water absorption of alginate based active films. Different letters indicate significantly differences in different films (P < 0.05).
(a) UV–Vis light transmittance curves and (b) digital photos of alginate based active films.
DPPH radical scavenging activity of alginate based active films.
(a) Antibacterial rate against E. coli and (b) digital photos of antibacterial activity for alginate based active films. SEM images of E. coli treated with (c) Alg and (d) LA-N1.
Gas barrier properties of alginate based active films (a, OTR. b, WVTR). Different letters indicate significantly differences in different films (P < 0.05).
Digital photos of bananas coated by alginate based active coatings on different days.
Mechanical properties of alginate based active films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Different letters in the same column indicate significantly differences in different films (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical parameters of alginate based active films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: * Transmittance at 274 and 600 nm. ** Absorbance at 600 nm. Different letters in the same column indicate significantly differences among films (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Alginate based films integrated with nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots and layered clay for active food packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xiamen Key Laboratory for Safety Evaluation and Testing of Food Packing Materials
Xiamen Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xiamen Key Laboratory for Safety Evaluation and Testing of Food Packing Materials, Xiamen Products Quality Supervision and Inspection Institute, Xiamen 361004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Packaging Design and Art, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou 412007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Packaging Design and Art

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Packaging Design and Art, Hunan University of Technology, Zhuzhou 412007, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The applications of alginate derived from seaweed polysaccharide in food packaging are restricted due to their inherent deficient antibacterial, antioxidant and UV barrier properties. In this study, nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots (NCDs) with active functions (0.5–3 %) and layered clay (1 %) with barrier property were introduced to construct alginate based active films via solution casting method. The results showed that the synthesized spherical NCDs had a particle size of 2–3 nm, and the internal structure of NCDs was similar to graphene, with a large number of active groups (-NH2, -OH, etc.) on the surface. Infrared analysis revealed that NCDs could form strong hydrogen bonds with alginate matrix, which slowed down the deterioration of mechanical properties and reduced the surface wettability. With the addition of NCDs, active functions and surface hydrophobicity of the active films were enhanced significantly (P < 0.05). When the amount of NCDs reached 3 %, UV barrier, antioxidant and antibacterial properties of the active films were increased by 50.0 %, 61.1 % and 70.1 %, respectively. The addition of NCDs could enhance the anti-browning ability of alginate based coatings and extend the shelf life of banana significantly. Therefore, a suitable amount of NCDs (1–2 %) and layered clay (1 %) can synergistically improve comprehensive performance of alginate based films and promote their food packaging application used as active films/inner coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The non-biodegradability and overuse of plastic materials in the food packaging industry have caused serious environmental pollution, which has a negative impact on human health and ecological environment [1]. Therefore, bio-based polymer materials with non-toxic, biodegradable and renewable properties are highly focused by researchers to develop active food packaging materials [2]. Meanwhile, they can also act as carriers for bioactive substances such as antioxidants or antimicrobials to prevent oxidation and spoilage of food [2].

Among these natural biodegradable polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids, etc.), alginate is an anionic polysaccharide derived from brown seaweeds that has received much attention for its biodegradability, non-toxicity, renewability, abundance and good film forming ability [3,4]. A large number of researchers have used alginate as a green, biodegradable matrix for active packaging films, active coatings, and edible coatings to extend food shelf life [5,6]. Regrettably, the biodegradable packaging materials prepared from pure alginate have some performance deficiencies, such as strong hydrophilicity, limited UV barrier and active packaging functions (antibacterial and antioxidant properties, etc.). In order to improve these properties, some effective improvements have been made. Several researchers have reported alginate blends with other polymers such as chitosan and its derivatives (chitosan biguanide and carboxymethyl cellulose, etc.) [1,7–9]. Another approach is to modify alginate matrix by introducing nano-fillers [5,6] (such as titanium oxide, chitin whiskers and silver loaded montmorillonite) or natural extracts [3,5,10] (such as green tea, aloe vera, and garlic).

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of nanomaterials, has attracted widespread attention due to its small particle size (<10 nm) and attractive properties such as photoluminescence, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and low-cost manufacturing [11,12]. CDs was first identified during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [11]. Since then, simple and efficient CDs can commonly be prepared from organic molecules (such as glucose) by bottom-up methods, and is widely used in various fields (optoelectronic devices, biomedical diagnosis and photocatalysis, etc.) [12,13]. CDs is composed of a chemical structure with a central carbon nucleus and edge functional groups, and the types of functional groups in CDs may vary depending on the carbon sources used. The mutual conjugation of core carbon dots with non-metallic elements (N, S, Si, etc.) has been proven to provide new functional groups for core CDs, thereby expanding their functions [14,15]. Among the possibilities, carbon sources containing N have made significant contributions due to their wider precursor range, naturally abundant sustainable resources, and simple synthesis route, resulting in various combinations of nitrogen functionalized carbon dots (NCDs) [16]. The presence of nitrogen functional groups such as amines can provide unexpected functions of CDs for matrix [17]. Therefore, NCDs has recently attracted the attention of researchers because of their strong antibacterial and antioxidant activity with low toxicity, and good dispersibility in aqueous suspensions [11,16]. In order to reduce the potential adverse effect of NCDs on the gas permeability of polymer materials, layered double hydroxides with high aspect ratios (LDHs, a kind of anionic layered clay nanosheets) has been used to modify polymer materials in our previous research, effectively improving gas barrier properties by extending the permeation path of gas molecules, especially oxygen and water molecules [18,19]. In addition, alginate matrix with strong hygroscopic properties are easy to become sticky and soft in humid environments, and the introduction of LDHs with high modulus can also increase the rigidity of alginate matrix.

In this study, we have explored the involvement of NCDs as active substance (antibacterial and antioxidant) into alginate based active food packaging materials for the first time. Meanwhile, the gas barrier properties and modulus of alginate matrix were improved by using layered clay naosheets (LDHs). For this, alginate based active films were developed by incorporating NCDs and LDHs via solution casting method. The effects of NCDs on the microscopic morphology, mechanical properties, hydrophilicity, optical properties, antibacterial, antioxidant and gas barrier properties of alginate based active films were studied for food packaging applications (especially for food active coatings).

Sodium alginate (Alg, purity≥90 %, M/G = 1:2), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH, purity = 96 %), methanol (purity = 99.5 %) were provided by Shanghai Makclin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Glucose (purity≥99.5 %), glycerin (purity≥99.7 %), urea (purity≥99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Layered clay (layered double hydroxides, LDHs) was synthesized by co-precipitation of metal salts and urea under hydrothermal conditions [20]. The main structural parameters of synthetic LDHs were presented in Supporting Information. Bananas were purchased from local fresh supermarket (Xiamen Sungiven Holding Foods Co., Ltd., China, grown in the Philippines). All reagents purchased were used directly without secondary treatment.

NCDs was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose and urea. In general, 1 g of glucose and 0.5 g of urea were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water and poured into a teflon-lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL). The reactor was heated to 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the reaction products were centrifuged (10,000 r/min) for 10 min to remove the unreacted material. Then, the supernatant was filtered by hydrophilic filtration membrane filter membrane (mixed cellulose, pore size: 0.22 μm, diameter: 50 mm, Jinteng, China), and the filtrate was further freeze-dried to obtain black NCDs, which was stored in the refrigerator for further use. The details on the characterization of NCDs were described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: Alginate based active films were fabricated by solution casting method. Briefly, 0.007 g of LDHs (1 % based on the mass of Alg) was evenly dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water, then 0.70 g of Alg and 0.21 g of glycerin (30 % based on the mass of Alg) were successively added into the above LDHs dispersion solution, and heated to 90 °C under continuous magnetic stirring, then cooled to 50 °C after continuous stirring for 30 min. A certain amount of NCDs was added into the above mixture respectively and stirred vigorously for 30 min. The obtained film-forming solution was ultrasonic for 5 min to remove bubbles, then poured into a horizontally placed teflon mold (5.5 cm × 5.0 cm), dried at 40 °C for 24 h, and conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % RH for 2 d before further testing. According to the mass fraction of NCDs in the alginate based active films (0, 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 %, and 3 %, based on the mass of Alg), the final alginate based active films were marked as LA, LA-N0.5, LA-N1, LA-N2, and LA-N3. The pure alginate with the same preparation process was marked as Alg. The methods for the characterization of alginate based active films were given in Supporting Information.

The significant differences in color parameters, opacity, mechanical properties, water contact angle, water absorption and gas barrier properties were determined with statistical analysis. The mean differences of alginate based active films were determined by analysis of variance (one-way ANOVA) in the data analysis module (Excel, Microsoft Office Professional Plus 2010). The level of significance was P < 0.05. All the figures were graphed with Origin 2018.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micromorphology and chemical structure of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the micromorphology and chemical structure of NCDs, the obtained NCDs is characterized by TEM, FT-IR and XPS, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 (a and b), NCDs is well dispersed spherical particles with diameters ranging from 1.6 nm to 4.0 nm, which is consistent with the diameter range (several nanometers) of general CDs [21,22]. And most NCDs range in diameter from 2 to 3 nm. HRTEM image (Fig. 1 inset) clearly shows crystalline structure. The interplanar spacing of lattice fringes is ∼0.21 nm, which reveals that NCDs has typical graphitic structure [23].

FT-IR measurements are conducted to obtain further structural insight into NCDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1c, there has a wide absorption band at 3660–3120 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs, which is related to the stretching vibration of -OH and NH [11,24]. The marked orange region is attributed to the stretching vibration peak with abundant methyl and methylene. The infrared absorption peaks at 1660, 1561, 1406 and 1070 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CC, the in-plane bending vibration of NH, the vibration of CN in the aromatic ring skeleton and the stretching vibration of C-O-C, respectively [25]. Compared with the infrared absorption peaks of urea and glucose, the infrared absorption characteristics of NCDs are completely different from those of raw materials, which indicates that a new chemical structure is generated under the condition of hydrothermal reaction.

XPS spectroscopy is further used to confirm the incorporation of nitrogen into the structure of NCDs, as shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2a, the elemental analysis results reveal that NCDs mainly consist of carbon (C1S, 284.8 eV), oxygen (O1S, 532.1 eV) and nitrogen (N1S, 399.7 eV) elements [14]. The relative contents of C, O and N on the surface of NCDs are 58.0 %, 27.6 %, and 14.4 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the high-resolution XPS is demonstrated to speculate the possible combination modes of C, O and N in NCDs. In detail, the spectrum of C 1S can be attributed to CC, CC, CH (284.6 eV), CO (285.8 eV), N-C=O (288.0 eV) bands, respectively (Fig. 2b) [14,22]. While the spectrum of O 1S in Fig. 2c can be attributed to CO (530.8 eV), CO, C-O-C (532.1 eV) bands [26,27]. In Fig. 2d, the spectrum of N 1S shows two peaks at 399.6 and 399.9 eV, corresponded to CN and NH, respectively [14]. Combined with HRTEM, FT-IR and XPS, it can reveal that NCDs is composed of internal graphene structure and external active groups (-NH2, -OH, etc.) [16].

Fluorescence spectrophotometer is used to conduct fluorescence test on NCDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 3a. The excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 400 nm, and the emission wavelength gradually shifts from 575 nm to 656 nm. Maximum emission wavelength is observed at 585–595 nm with an excitation wavelength of 295–300 nm. This reveals that the photoluminescence (PL) of NCDs is closely related to the excitation wavelength, indicating that surface defects and abundant functional groups may be related to the PL process [11,28]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the UV–Vis spectrum exhibits a absorption peak at around 274 nm, which is attributed to the typical absorption of π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [16,29]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of NCDs are around 295 and 585 nm, respectively. However, different from the literature reports, the peak width of excitation and emission spectrum of NCDs is very narrow, which may cause that the PL effect of NCDs cannot be excited with conventional ultraviolet lamp wavelength (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectroscopy is used to study the chemical structure of alginate based active films, as shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that Alg shows all the infrared adsorption characteristic peaks of alginate reported in the literature [1,2]. The absorption peak at 3246 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH, and the absorption peaks at 1594 and 1404 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl groups, respectively [6]. The absorption peak corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO in the ring structure is observed at 1304 cm−1. In addition, the absorption bands at 1083 and 1021 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of C-O-C in the ring structure [3]. With the addition of NCDs, there is no significant change between alginate based active films and Alg. However, it is worth noting that the infrared absorption peak of the stretching vibration for -OH in the alginate based active films shows red shift from 3257 cm−1 to 3229 cm−1, which is due to the introduction of NH groups in NCDs, leading to the stretching vibration of -OH and NH at 3229 cm−1 and the formation of internal hydrogen bond association.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and hydrophilicity of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: SEM is used to investigate the surface and fracture surface of alginate based active films, as shown in Fig. 5 (a-c), Fig. 1S and Fig. 2S. In Fig. 5a, the surface of Alg is smooth. In Fig. 5b, LDHs is not well dispersed in the alginate matrix and exhibit surface protrusions in certain parts of LA, especially the hexagonal layered structure of LDHs appearing in the marked area. After the addition of NCDs (Fig. 5c), the surface of LA-N1 shows less obvious protrusions. Combining Fig. 5(a-c) and Fig. 1S, it can be seen that with the addition of NCDs, the degree of LDHs protrusions significantly decreases, indicating that the introduction of NCDs improves the interfacial compatibility between alginate matrix and LDHs. EDS analysis is further conducted on the surface of LA-N1 in Fig. 5d. It is found that in addition to the component elements (C, O and Na) of alginate, there is also uniformly distributed N element, which indicates that NCDs is evenly dispersed in the alginate matrix. In Fig. 2S, it can be seen that LDHs is observed in the fracture morphology of LA, and the layered structure of LDHs does not undergo damage or deformation during the preparation process. Due to the nanoscale nature of NCDs and their excellent dispersibility in water, the presence and dispersion of NCDs cannot be observed in the fracture surface of LA-N1. In addition, the fracture surface of LA and LA-N1 maintains good compactness without visible bubbles.

Water contact angle and water absorption of alginate based active films are tested, as shown in Fig. 5 (e and f). Surface tension characterization through water contact angle measurements is a good way to determine the hydrophilicity of the film [30]. It can be seen from Fig. 5e that water contact angle of Alg is only 65.7°. With the addition of LDHs, water contact angle of LA increases to 73.5°, which indicates that the presence of LDHs causes the decrease of hydrophilicity of LA surface. When the amount of NCDs is <1 %, water contact angle of LA-N1 further increases significantly (P < 0.05), reaching the maximum value of 108.2°. From the structural analysis of NCDs, it is known that a abundant active groups on NCDs can easily form new hydrogen bonds with alginate matrix and LDHs, thus achieving the physical cross-linking, and ultimately hindering the wetting of water molecules at the beginning of contact [31,32]. With the addition of NCDs, water contact angle of alginate based active films begins to decrease. When the amount of NCDs reaches 3 %, water contact angle of LA-N3 decreases to 75.2°, indicating that excessive addition of hydrophilic NCDs leads to an increase in the surface hydrophilicity [11]. Even though the higher content of NCDs leads to a significant decrease (P < 0.05) in water contact angle of alginate based active films, it is still higher than that of Alg. The results show that the addition of LDHs and NCDs can improve the hydrophilicity of alginate matrix.

As shown in Fig. 5f, water absorption of Alg reaches 8.7 %, and water absorption of LA decreases to 3.8 % significantly (P < 0.05) when 1 % LDHs is added. Combined with water contact angle test results, this is partly due to a decrease in the surface hydrophilicity, and on the other hand, the presence of layered clay nanosheets reduces the permeability of water vapor [33]. When the addition of NCDs reaches 0.5 %, water absorption of LA-N0.5 increases to 6.0 %. Although water absorption of alginate based active films shows a continuous upward trend due to the hydrophilicity of NCDs, water absorption does not change significantly (P > 0.05) with the increase of NCDs. When the addition of NCDs reaches 3 %, water absorption (7.0 %) of LA-N3 is still lower than that of Alg. Therefore, the presence of LDHs nanosheets can reduce the influence of NCDs on the water absorption of alginate matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanical properties of alginate based active films are presented in Table 1 . The addition of LDHs does not significantly (P > 0.05) reduce the tensile strength of alginate matrix, but it still decreases from 18.0 MPa (Alg) to 16.9 MPa (LA). Combined with the surface morphology analysis of alginate based active films, this may be related to the poor interfacial compatibility and partial agglomeration of LDHs [34]. When the amount of NCDs is between 0.5 % and 2 %, tensile strength of alginate based active films has no significant change (P > 0.05). When the amount of NCDs reaches 3 %, tensile strength is reduced by 21.7 % compared with Alg. The addition of inorganic LDHs causes a significant decrease (P < 0.05) in elongation at break of LA, which is due to the poor interfacial adhesion between alginate matrix and LDHs [20,34]. Similar to the variation pattern of tensile strength, elongation at break of alginate based active films (0.5–2 % NCDs) has no significant change (P > 0.05). Although there is no significant difference in the tensile strength and elongation at break after the addition of NCDs (0.5–2 %), there is still a slight improvement trend. When the amount of NCDs reaches a certain level (3 %), it will cause a significant decrease in mechanical properties. Combined with the surface morphology and FT-IR analysis, this may be related to the fact that the addition of NCDs can form new hydrogen bonds to improve the interfacial compatibility between LDHs and alginate matrix [11].

Different from the change trend of tensile strength and elongation at break, the presence of LDHs with high modulus significantly (P < 0.05) increases the tensile modulus of LA [35]. With the addition of NCDs, tensile modulus of alginate based active films shows an obvious trend (P < 0.05) of decline (compared with LA). However, when the amount of NCDs is between 0.5 % and 1 %, the tensile modulus (565.3–404.9 MPa) of alginate based active films is still higher than that of Alg (351.3 MPa). It is worth noting that the addition of NCDs has little effect on the tensile strength and elongation at break of alginate based active films, but significantly reduces the tensile modulus, resulting in a decrease in the stiffness. In the future, alginate based active films with appropriate amount of NCDs (0.5 %–1 %) can be used as food contact active coatings on the traditional packaging films (as inner coatings) or fresh food, so mechanical properties are not as high as the requirements of conventional packaging films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: Excellent UV barrier properties and visible light transmittance are essential properties for transparent active packaging films [36,37]. Fig. 6 shows UV–Vis light transmittance curves and digital photos of alginate based active films. It can be seen from Fig. 6a that both Alg and LA maintain good transmittance. UV barrier properties can effectively slow down the oxidation of nutrients (vitamins and lipids, etc.) in food, and maintain the food flavor [38]. With the addition of NCDs, UV–vis spectra of alginate based active films changes obviously. Especially in the UV range (190–400 nm), the UV transmittance decreases significantly due to UV absorption of NCDs. When the amount of NCDs reaches 3 %, the UV transmittance (274 nm) is only 6.6 %, which is 50.0 % lower than that of Alg (56.6 %). In the visible range (400–800 nm), when the amount of NCDs reaches 0.5 %, the transmittance at 600 nm of LA-N0.5 decreases to 62.9 %, which is only 15.5 % lower than that of Alg. With the addition of NCDs, there is no significant change in the visible transmittance (in the range of 62.3–65.9 %). It reveals that the addition of NCDs has a much higher barrier effect for UV light than visible light, so that alginate based active films can play an excellent UV barrier properties without affecting the visibility.

As seen from digital photos of alginate based active films (Fig. 6b), the color changes from colorless to yellowish brown with the addition of NCDs. However, all the films maintain good transparency, and the graphics behind the films are clearly displayed. According to the absorbance at 600 nm and the thickness of each film, the opacity of alginate based active films can be calculated as shown in Table 2 . The opacity value of all the films remains relatively low, which indicates that all the films are transparent, the phenomenon is consistent with that observed in the digital photos [39]. In order to further study the specific color trend of alginate based active films. The colorimeter is used for color analysis. With the addition of NCDs, the value of L* significantly decreases (P < 0.05), and the value of a* and b* significantly increases (P < 0.05), which indicates a significant decrease in brightness of alginate based active films with a clear trend towards yellowing and reddening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: As an important indicator of active packaging materials, antioxidant activity can effectively protect the nutritional substances of food from oxidation and deterioration, and maintain the quality of food [39,40]. DPPH radical scavenging experiments are used to evaluate the antioxidant activity of alginate based active films, as shown in Fig. 7 . DPPH radical scavenging rate of NCDs reaches 76.45 %, showing good antioxidant activity, which is due to the abundant active groups of NCDs [26,41]. DPPH radical scavenging rate of Alg is only 6.19 %, indicating poor antioxidant activity, which may be attributed to antioxidant activity of a small number of active sites. The addition of LDHs hinders the release of gelatin peptide, resulting in the reduction of LA to 1.77 %. With the addition of NCDs, DPPH radical scavenging rate of alginate based active films increases gradually. However, the overall increase in antioxidant activity of alginate based active films is limited, which may be due to the low purity of NCDs. When the amount of NCDs reaches 3 %, DPPH radical scavenging rate of alginate based active films reaches the maximum of 67.26 %, which is increased by 61.07 % compared with Alg. Therefore, the presence of NCDs can significantly improve the antioxidant activity of alginate matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial activity is also a necessary property of active packaging materials, which inhibits the proliferation of microorganisms and prolongs the shelf life of food [36,42]. Antibacterial rate against E. coli and digital photos antibacterial activity for alginate based active films are shown in Fig. 8 (a and b). In Fig. 8a, NCDs has excellent antibacterial activity. This is because CDs can be adsorbed to the cell walls of bacteria and fungi through diffusion and electrostatic action, thus preventing their uptake of nutrients [26,43]. Meanwhile, CDs can also penetrate cell membranes, inhibit the formation of bacterial biofilms, and lead to cytoplasmic leakage [43]. Alg and LA show no obvious antibacterial activity. After the addition of NCDs, antibacterial activity of alginate based active films is significantly improved. When the addition of NCDs reaches 3 %, the number of E. coli colonies is decreased from 3.18 × 108 CFU∙mL−1 (Blank sample) to 1.8 × 107 CFU∙mL−1, and the antibacterial rate reaches 94.3 %.

The E. coli colonies in the antibacterial rate test are further extracted and fixed, and SEM is applied to observe the micromorphology of bacteria, as shown in Fig. 8(c and d). In Fig. 8c, E. coli treated with Alg shows a typical rod-like shape, complete cell structure and smooth surface [11]. After the addition of NCDs (1 %), a large number of bacteria are broken and structurally incomplete, which indicates that NCDs can significantly hinder the reproduction and metabolism of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas barrier properties of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: Gas barrier properties of alginate based active films are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that the oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of Alg are only 39.4 cm3/(m2·24h·0.1MP) and 6.5 g/(m2·24h), respectively. OTR and WVTR of commercially available biodegradable polylactic acid (PLA) film (Thickness: 30 μm) in our previous studies are 670–765 cm3/(m2·24h·0.1MP) and 184–222 g/(m2·24h), respectively [18,19], which indicates that gas barrier properties of Alg is significantly superior to that of PLA film. The oxygen and water vapor barrier properties of LA are improved by 19.8 % and 15.3 % (P < 0.05) compared with Alg, respectively. According to literature reports, the introduction of CDs can enhance the interface compatibility between nanoparticles and polymer matrix, reduce the interchain space in polymer structure, and hinder the permeation of gas molecules [11]. Unlike the reports, the addition of NCDs slight decreases the gas barrier properties of alginate based active films. Even though, when the amount of NCDs reaches 3 %, OTR of LA-N3 is still lower (P < 0.05) than that of Alg, and WVTR is slightly higher (P > 0.05) than that of Alg. The slight increase in WTVR may be due to the addition of hydrophilic NCDs affecting the hydrophilicity of alginate matrix, making it easier for water molecules to penetrate the film [2,11]. Therefore, the barrier effect of layered clay significantly reduce gas permeability, which can compensate for the decrease in gas barrier properties of NCDs on the alginate matrix [44]. Due to their poor mechanical properties and excellent gas barrier properties, alginate based active materials have more practical value in the future as coatings on conventional films or food surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fresh-keeping property of alginate based active films
ce:section.ce:para: Reducing the undesirable browning process has gained much interest since it lowers the quality and nutritive value of fruits and freshcut vegetables [45]. The browning process has been attributed mainly to the action of polyphenoloxidase (PPO) on polyphenols producing quinones that are responsible for the developed color under aerobic conditions [45,46]. Fresh bananas are taken as the object of this study, and the browning process of these bananas over time after coated by different active coatings is recorded, so as to verify fresh-keeping application potential of alginate based active films/coatings in the field of fresh food, as shown in Fig. 10 . It can be seen that the blank sample shows obvious black spots on the third day, but on the fifth day it has softened and turned black overall. The bananas coated with Alg and LA maintain a similar phenomenon, and even on the fifth day, the browning phenomenon is not as severe as the blank sample on the third day. This indicates that alginate matrix has good oxygen barrier properties that can reduce oxygen contact and slow down browning. The bananas coated with LA-N1 exhibit excellent anti-browning ability, with no obvious browning spots even on the fifth day, which is closely related to the excellent antioxidant activity of NCDs. The results indicate that with the antioxidant activity of NCDs and the excellent barrier properties of alginate matrix and LDHs, the active coatings can effectively slow down the browning rate of bananas.

In this study, NCDs was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose and urea. N-functionalized carbon dots (NCDs) with active functions (0.5–3 %) and layered clay (1 %) with barrier properties were introduced to construct alginate based active films via solution casting method. The results showed that the synthesized spherical NCDs had a particle size of 2–3 nm, and the internal structure of NCDs was similar to graphene, with a large number of active groups (-NH2, -OH, etc.) on the surface. With the addition of NCDs, active packaging functions and and surface hydrophobicity of alginate based active films were enhanced significantly, but gas barrier properties were damaged slightly. When the amount of NCDs reached 3 %, UV barrier, antioxidant and antibacterial properties of alginate based active films were increased by 50.0 %, 61.1 % and 70.1 %, respectively. The presence of layered clay could improve the gas barrier property of alginate matrix by extending the permeability path of gas molecules, thus reducing the deterioration of gas barrier properties of alginate matrix by NCDs. The addition of NCDs could enhance the anti-browning ability of alginate based coatings and extend the shelf life of bananas significantly. Therefore, due to their poor mechanical properties, excellent gas barrier properties and active packaging functions, alginate based materials integrated with a suitable amount of NCDs (1–2 %) and layered clay (1 %) can be used as active films/inner coatings for food packaging applications.

Long Mao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Juan Zuo: Data curation, Resources. Yujie Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Bowen Zheng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xianglong Dai: Software. Zeqing Bai: Investigation. Yulin Liu: Investigation. Jin Yao: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52103098), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province, China (No. 2021J05265), Open fund of Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications (Xiamen University of Technology) (No. fma2023006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.126653.
